(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17年度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]]== See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| shrines]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] Shrine]][[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] }} [[Category:11]][[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17年度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] }} [[Category:11]][[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:11]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| shrines]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted]][[Category: Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:refreshing [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] [[sn:eguro Gokoku Shrine]]

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館|12=draft}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|1=Meguro Gokoku Shrine|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|12=draft}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine|Meguro Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|1=Meguro Gokoku Shrine|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|12=draft}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館|12=draft}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] [[sn:eguro Gokoku Shrine]]

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|1=Meguro Gokoku Shrine|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|12=draft}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館|12=draft}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Unsorted]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] [[sn:eguro Gokoku Shrine]]

The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館|12=draft}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] }} [[en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] [[sn:eguro Gokoku Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine|Meguro Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|1=Meguro Gokoku Shrine|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|12=draft}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木|12=draft}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館|12=draft}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{afc comment|1=see [[every:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] }} [[en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] [[sn:eguro Gokoku Shrine]]

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Meguro Gokoku Shrine | image = {{image requested|cat=目黒区}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|coord={{Coord|35|38|4.04|N|139|41|21.24|E|format=dms}}|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|frame-height=250}} | location = {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan | latitude = 35.634456 | longitude = 139.689233 | deities = Approximately 2,300 war dead from the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] onward | founded = 1941 (Showa 16) | festival = | structure = | class = Non-designated Gokoku Shrine }} '''Meguro Gokoku Shrine''' ({{nihongo|目黒護國神社|1=Meguro Gokoku Shrine|Meguro Gokoku Jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Gohongi, Meguro|ja|五本木}}, {{ill|Meguro, Tokyo|ja|目黒区}}, {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, Japan. It was a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal|last=TAKAYAMA|first=K. PETER|date=1990|title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081|journal=Journal of Church and State|volume=32|issue=3|pages=527–547|issn=0021-969X}} == History == === Establishment === The shrine was established in March 1941, during the early [[Showa era]], following a donation of residential land and part of a house by the family of Zenbei Moriya, the former president of the Taiwan Nichi Nichi Shinpō newspaper, with the condition that a shrine honoring fallen war heroes be built. The construction of the shrine was funded by public expenses.{{Cite book |url=https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/67/h17.pdf |title=平成17th year 度 包括外部監査の結果報告書 |publisher=Meguro City |date=2006-02 |pages=111-112}}{{Cite news |title=戦没者ほこらひっそり幕 目黒護国神社、管理者なく |newspaper=Tokyo Shimbun |date=2008-07-08 |edition=Evening}} After [[World War II]] and the implementation of the [[Japanese Constitution]], Meguro City could no longer operate the shrine due to the [[separation of church and state]]. Management was transferred to the bereaved families of war victims. In 1958, the shrine was relocated within the grounds of the Meguro Public Library due to renovations. === Abolition === By 2000, the shrine's managing association had become inactive following the deaths of key members. This resulted in unpaid land rent and the disappearance of the war dead registry. In 2005, an external audit cited not only governance concerns but also safety issues due to the aging structure. In July 2008, the shrine was dismantled during the demolition of the adjacent Moriya Educational Hall. Attempts to transfer its management or merge it with other shrines were unsuccessful. The former site of the shrine is now occupied by a [[disaster preparedness storage facility]], while the Moriya Educational Hall was replaced by a private nursery school.[https://www.city.meguro.tokyo.jp/documents/623/mineikakeikaku_1.pdf 区立保育園の民営化に関する計画] - Meguro City == location == * Address: 2-20-17 Gohongi, Meguro City, Tokyo, Japan == Timeline == * 1940 (Showa 15) ''' July: Donation of Zenbei Moriya's former residence with conditions to establish the shrine ''' September: Donation approved * 1941 (Showa 16) ''' March: Shrine established using public funds ''' April: Land ownership registered ''' December: Moriya Memorial Hall (later Meguro Public Library) opened * 1958 (Showa 33): Shrine relocated within the Moriya Memorial Hall grounds during library renovations * 1959 (Showa 34): Meguro Gokoku Shrine Supporters' Association formed * 2000 (Heisei 12): Shrine management became inactive * 2008 (Heisei 20): Shrine dismantled == Related Articles == * {{ill|Meguro Public Library|ja|目黒区立図書館}} - Constructed on the site of Zenbei Moriya's former residence, which included the Meguro Gokoku Shrine. {{citation needed|Meguro Gokoku Shrine}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku shrines}} }} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Meguro]] [[Category:Former shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Tokyo]] [[Category:War memorials in Japan]] [[Category:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category: Completion tier 2]] [[Category: categories]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Meguro Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:目黒護國神社]] [[sn:eguro Gokoku Shrine]]
